items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: Why are top predators most affected by persistent pollutants like mercury?
items.correct: Biomagnification concentrates pollutants at each trophic level up the food chain.
items.misconception: Predators are exposed to more pollution directly.
items.why: Students focus on direct exposure. The mechanism is concentration through eating contaminated prey - small accumulations in lower levels multiply upward, so apex predators have the highest body burdens.

items.q: What is the difference between species richness and species evenness?
items.correct: Richness counts how many species exist; evenness measures how balanced their abundances are.
items.random_wrong: Richness is for plants, evenness for animals.
items.why: Students conflate these. A community with 10 species each having 100 individuals has high evenness; one with 10 species but 991 of species A and 1 of each other has low evenness despite identical richness.
The answer examiners want is: Soil pH. “Predators” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An organism's role in an ecosystem is its?
items.why: It's Niche. “Habitat” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many trophic levels does a typical food chain have at minimum?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 levels. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
